coredata.dc:title: Quantum yield of carbon dots adjusts the performance of TiO2-In2S3-based photoelectrochemical biosensor for alpha-fetoprotein detection
coredata.dc:creator: Zhao, Hongxu
Qi, Haiyan
Yi, Tonghui
Jing, Tao
Li, Jun
Shen, Siqi
Zeng, Qingxin
Gao, Yang
coredata.dc:description: The development of highly efficient photoelectrochemical (PEC) biosensors is crucial for sensitive detection in various applications, yet it is often impeded by rapid electron-hole recombination, which limits charge separation efficiency. In this study, we developed a novel PEC biosensor based on carbon dots (CDs) to address this challenge. The TiO2-In2S3-CDs PEC biosensor was optimized by adjusting reaction time, temperature, and precursor concentration to achieve optimal performance. Detailed analyses, including XPS, UV, SEM, EDS, and EIS, confirmed the successful integration of CDs and the impact of their quantum yield (QY) on the photoelectric properties of the biosensor. Our results indicated that higher QY in CDs correlates with enhanced photoelectric performance, as evidenced by a reduction in the band gap from 3.06 eV to 2.61 eV. The underlying mechanism was attributed to the presence of abundant oxygen-containing surface groups and graphitic nitrogen in high-QY CDs, which facilitated efficient electron transfer and enhanced PEC performance. The optimized TiO2-In2S3-CDs PEC biosensor demonstrated excellent photoelectric properties and was successfully applied for the detection of alpha-fetoprotein (AFP), achieving a detection limit (LOD) of 5.1 pg/mL. Furthermore, our PEC biosensor demonstrated superior selectivity, anti-interference properties, stability, and reproducibility, making it a promising tool for AFP detection in clinical applications. The study highlights the potential of QY-enhanced CDs in advancing PEC biosensor performance and offers a promising strategy for the development of next-generation biosensors with improved sensitivity and selectivity.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Quantum yield
Carbon dots
PEC biosensor performance
AFP detection

Photocatalytic mechanism and functional group analysis
Impact of quantum yield on photocatalytic properties of CDs
Electrochemical and EIS testing of the PEC biosensor
Performance evaluation of the TiO2-In2S3-CDs PEC biosensor for AFP detection
Selectivity, anti-interference, stability, and reproducibility testing of the TiO2-In2S3-CDs PEC biosensor

(a) TEM of TiO2; (b) SEM of TiO2-In2S3.

(a) UV-DRS spectra of TiO2, TiO2-In2S3, and TiO2-In2S3-CDs; (b) Band gap calculation of TiO2, TiO2-In2S3, and TiO2-In2S3-CDs; (c) Photocatalytic mechanism of the TiO2-In2S3-CDs PEC biosensor for AFP detection.

(a) Correlation between photocurrent intensity and the QY of different CDs; (b) Correlation between QYs and oxygen (CO/CO) and nitrogen groups on surface of CDs; (c) Zeta potential measurements of all four CDs.

(a) Photocurrent responses of TiO2-In2S3-CDs-aptamer-BSA-AFP(a), TiO2 (b), TiO2-In2S3-CDs-aptamer-BSA(c),TiO2-In2S3 (d), TiO2-In2S3-CDs-aptamer(e), and TiO2-In2S3-CDs (f); (b) EIS Nyquist plot of TiO2-In2S3-CDs-aptamer-BSA-AFP(a), TiO2 (b), TiO2-In2S3-CDs-aptamer-BSA(c), TiO2-In2S3 (d), TiO2-In2S3-CDs-aptamer(e), and TiO2-In2S3-CDs (f).
Proportions, Reaction Times, Temperatures, and Corresponding Fluorescence Quantum Yields (QY) of CDs Synthesis.
y of AFP Detection in Spiked Human Serum Samples.

head.ce:title: Quantum yield of carbon dots adjusts the performance of TiO2-In2S3-based photoelectrochemical biosensor for alpha-fetoprotein detection

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Heilongjiang Provincial Key Laboratory of Surface Active Agent and Auxiliary, Qiqihar University, Qiqihar 161006, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The development of highly efficient photoelectrochemical (PEC) biosensors is crucial for sensitive detection in various applications, yet it is often impeded by rapid electron-hole recombination, which limits charge separation efficiency. In this study, we developed a novel PEC biosensor based on carbon dots (CDs) to address this challenge. The TiO2-In2S3-CDs PEC biosensor was optimized by adjusting reaction time, temperature, and precursor concentration to achieve optimal performance. Detailed analyses, including XPS, UV, SEM, EDS, and EIS, confirmed the successful integration of CDs and the impact of their quantum yield (QY) on the photoelectric properties of the biosensor. Our results indicated that higher QY in CDs correlates with enhanced photoelectric performance, as evidenced by a reduction in the band gap from 3.06 eV to 2.61 eV. The underlying mechanism was attributed to the presence of abundant oxygen-containing surface groups and graphitic nitrogen in high-QY CDs, which facilitated efficient electron transfer and enhanced PEC performance. The optimized TiO2-In2S3-CDs PEC biosensor demonstrated excellent photoelectric properties and was successfully applied for the detection of alpha-fetoprotein (AFP), achieving a detection limit (LOD) of 5.1 pg/mL. Furthermore, our PEC biosensor demonstrated superior selectivity, anti-interference properties, stability, and reproducibility, making it a promising tool for AFP detection in clinical applications. The study highlights the potential of QY-enhanced CDs in advancing PEC biosensor performance and offers a promising strategy for the development of next-generation biosensors with improved sensitivity and selectivity.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Alpha-fetoprotein (AFP) is a well-recognized tumor marker for various cancers, particularly associated with primary liver cancer, such as hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC) [1,2]. AFP is a glycoprotein typically produced by the fetal liver, yolk sac, and gastrointestinal tract, with its levels declining after birth in healthy individuals. Elevated AFP levels in adults are commonly associated with malignant transformation of hepatocytes, making AFP a critical marker for the detection and monitoring of liver cancer [3,4]. Traditional AFP detection methods, including enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA), radioimmunoassay (RIA), and chemiluminescent immunoassay, are effective but often time-consuming and require specialized equipment [5]. Thus, developing a rapid and convenient detection method for AFP is of considerable clinical importance. Photochemistry offers a promising alternative by integrating electrochemical and optical techniques to enhance both sensitivity and speed [6]. Recent advancements in photochemistry have led to the development of PEC biosensors, which provide real-time analysis with high accuracy [7].

Titanium dioxide (TiO2) is a widely studied in photochemistry due to its excellent optical properties and chemical stability [8]. As an n-type semiconductor, TiO2 facilitates charge separation by accepting electrons. However, its catalytic efficiency is often limited by the high recombination rate of electron-hole pairs [9,10]. To overcome this limitation, researchers have employed strategies such as surface doping with metals or non-metals, catalyst loading, and heterojunction formation. These modifications aim to enhance TiO2's photocatalytic performance by reducing recombination rates and improving charge carrier dynamics [11].

Loading catalysts onto TiO2 has been shown to significantly enhance its photocatalytic performance. Commonly used loading materials include various sulfides, oxides, and metals. For instance, transition metal sulfides like molybdenum disulfide (MoS2) and tungsten disulfide (WS2) have demonstrated strong photocatalytic activity due to their unique electronic properties and ability to absorb light effectively [12]. Additionally, oxides such as iron oxide (Fe2O3) and zinc oxide (ZnO) are frequently used to modify TiO2, improving its photocatalytic capabilities by extending its absorption spectrum and enhancing charge separation. The strategic choice of loading catalysts is crucial in optimizing the photocatalytic properties of TiO2 [13,14].

Carbon dots (CDs) have emerged as a highly promising material in the field of photocatalysis and sensing due to their exceptional luminescence and electron transfer capabilities [15]. CDs are nanoparticles with sizes ranging from 1 to 10 nm and possess unique optical and electronic properties that enhance their photocatalytic performance [16]. They can be synthesized through top-down or bottom-up approaches, with the top-down method facilitating the integration of sp2 conjugated domains within the CDs. Additionally, CDs doped with elements like nitrogen or sulfur can further enhance their electron mobility and photocatalytic activity [17]. The incorporation of CDs into PEC biosensor materials has been shown to significantly improve photoelectric performance, making them valuable in developing highly sensitive and efficient biosensors [18,19].

In this study, we developed an innovative PEC biosensor featuring CDs as the core sensing element. The TiO2-In2S3-CDs PEC biosensor was systematically optimized to enhance photocatalytic activity and sensitivity specifically for AFP detection (Fig. 1 ). We explored the impact of CDs' quantum yield (QY) on the biosensor’s photoelectric performance and thoroughly assessed its selectivity, interference resistance, stability, and reproducibility. The biosensor's efficacy was further validated using real samples, demonstrating its robust capability for accurate AFP detection. In conclusion, the developed PEC biosensor offered a highly sensitive and reliable method for AFP detection, leveraging the enhanced photoelectric properties of CDs and demonstrating promising potential for clinical applications.

Deionized water was used throughout the experiment. Acetone, ammonia, ammonium persulfate, butyl titanate, chitosan (CS), diethylene glycol (DEG), ethanol, glucose, hydrochloric acid, potassium chloride, sodium chloride, tetrabutyl titanate, and thiourea, were obtained from Tianjin Kaitong Chemical Reagent Co., LTD., and were of analytical grade. Alpha-fetoprotein (AFP), ascorbic acid (AA), bovine serum albumin (BSA), carcinoembryonic antigen (CEA), citric acid, disodium hydrogen phosphate, glutaraldehyde, immunoglobulin (IgG), indium nitrate, indium nitrate tetrahydrate, L-cysteine, potassium dihydrogen phosphate, potassium ferricyanide, potassium ferrocyanide, prostate antigen (PSA), and serum were purchased from Sinopharm chemical reagent co., LTD. Fluor-doped tin oxide (FTO) conductive glass was selected as the catalyst substrate.

The TiO2-In2S3-CDs PEC biosensor was synthesized through a multi-step process. The procedure began with the preparation of the TiO2 photocatalyst. A mixture of hydrochloric acid, deionized water, and butyl titanate was prepared in a 30:30:1 vol ratio. The mixture was then transferred into an autoclave vessel containing FTO glass, which was subsequently placed in an oven at 150 °C for 6 h. This process facilitated the formation of a uniform TiO2 layer on the surface of the FTO glass, providing a robust foundation for the subsequent steps.

Next, CDs were synthesized. 1.0 g of citric acid and 1 g of L-cysteine were dissolved in 100 mL of deionized water and stirred for 15 min at room temperature, 0.02 g of ammonium persulfate was then added to the solution, and the pH was adjusted to neutral using ammonia. The solution was transferred into an autoclave vessel and heated at 180 °C for 5 h to synthesize CDs. The prepared CDs were purified by dialysis using a membrane with a molecular weight cutoff (MWCO) of 500 Da for 24 h, followed by drying to yield pure CDs.

For the final step, the TiO2-In2S3-CDs PEC biosensor was synthesized by first dissolving 0.87 g of indium nitrate tetrahydrate and 2.30 g of thiourea in a mixture of 8.0 mL of deionized water and 52 mL of DEG, followed by stirring for 30 min at room temperature. Subsequently, 0.05 mg of the previously synthesized CDs was added to the mixture. The resulting solution was then poured into an autoclave vessel containing the TiO2-coated FTO glass. The reaction was conducted at 140 °C for 3 h, allowing the TiO2-In2S3-CDs PEC biosensor to form on the FTO substrate. The FTO glass was then removed from an autoclave vessel, yielding the final PEC biosensor.

50.00 μL of 0.1 % CS solution was drip-coated on a electrode with an area of 1.0 cm2, and was completely dried at room temperature. The composite electrode was placed in 5 mL of 2.5 % glutaraldehyde crosslinker and soaked for 1 h at room temperature. 50 μL of 2 μM AFP aptamer solution was added dropwise to to the TiO2-In2S3-CDs electrode surface and then incubated at 37 °C for 3 h. The electrodes was washed with TE buffer solution (pH = 7.4) to remove excess unbound aptamer. The electrode was incubated in 10 mL of BSA (3 %, wt) for 1 h to block non-specific binding sites, and finally the electrode was rinsed by PBS buffer solution (pH = 7.4). The prepared PEC aptamer biosensor was labeled as TiO2-In2S3-CDs and stored in 4 °C environment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photoelectrochemical (PEC) and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) testing
ce:section.ce:para: The PEC properties of the TiO2-In2S3-CDs PEC biosensor were evaluated using a three-electrode system in a 0.1 M phosphate-buffered saline (PBS) solution at pH 7.4. A 500 W xenon lamp (PEC4000, Beijing NBET, China) equipped with a 400 nm cutoff filter served as the light source, providing an intensity of approximately 50 mW cm−2. The photocurrent density was measured using I-t technology at a constant potential of 0.3 V vs. Ag/AgCl, allowing for the real-time assessment of the biosensor's photoelectric response. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was also performed to evaluate the charge transfer resistance and overall stability of the TiO2-In2S3-CDs composite.

The synthesis of CDs was systematically optimized by varying reaction time, temperature, and the ratio of raw materials. The fluorescence quantum yield (QY) of each sample was measured to determine the optimal conditions (Table 1 ).The calculation method for QY of CDs was presented in supplementary material. Among the tested conditions, the highest QY of 32.35 % was achieved with a reaction time of 5 h, a temperature of 180 °C, and a citric acid to L-cysteine of 1:1 mass ratio. This optimized batch of CDs, designated as CDs 3, demonstrated superior optical properties, and was selected for use in preparing subsequent PEC biosensor materials.

High resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) images of CDs 3 revealed that the CDs have a small and uniform particle size with an average diameter of 1.5 nm, and a size distribution ranging from 0.5 to 3.0 nm (Fig. 2 a-b). The uniformity suggested that CDs exhibited high stability and resistance to decomposition, which are essential for reliable performance in photoelectric applications.

The electrochemical performance of the TiO2-In2S3-CDs PEC biosensor was then evaluated (Fig. 2c). Pure TiO2 exhibited a relatively low photocurrent due to its limited photocarrier mobility. The incorporation of indium sulfide (In2S3) into the PEC biosensor significantly enhanced the photocurrent. This improvement is attributed to the reduction in energy level spacing and the redshift in the absorption band, which leads to a decreased bandgap and increased photocarrier generation. The addition of CDs 3 further increased the photocurrent to 110 μA, underscoring the pivotal role of CDs in facilitating electron transfer and minimizing electron-hole recombination [20]. The effect of varying the amount of CDs on the photocurrent was also investigated (Fig. 2d). The optimal enhancement in photocurrent was observed with the addition of 0.5 mg of CDs 3. At lower concentrations, the CDs effectively captured electrons, boosting the current. However, higher concentration of CDs led to reduced photocurrent due to excessive CDs impeding electron movement [21]. Additionally, the optimization of reaction time and temperature for the TiO2-In2S3-CDs PEC biosensor was further explored (Figs. S1 and S2), providing further insights into the photoelectric performance of the PEC biosensor under different conditions.

The structural and compositional properties of the TiO2-In2S3-CDs PEC biosensor were analyzed using SEM and TEM. TEM image of TiO2 revealed its rod-shaped morphology (Fig. 3 a). This rod-like structure provided a large specific surface area, enhancing the material's capacity to support additional substances and potentially improving its photocatalytic efficiency by mitigating limitations associated with its narrow band gap [22]. In contrast, the SEM image of the TiO2-In2S3 PEC biosensor exhibited a nano-flower structure with extensive internal cavities (Fig. 3b). The nano-flower morphology, characterized by significant internal gaps, facilitated better interaction with other substances and created multiple channels for current-carrier transfer, which likely enhanced the material’s photocatalytic activity [23]. The energy dispersive spectrometer (EDS) of the TiO2-In2S3-CDs PEC biosensor confirmed the presence of C, In , O, N, S and Ti indicating the successful doping of CDs into the PEC biosensor (Fig. S3). This elemental composition suggested a well-suited material for improving photoelectric properties through synergistic effects.

Ultraviolet–visible diffuse reflectance spectroscopy (UV–ViS) was employed to assess the optical properties of the materials (Fig. 4 a). TiO2 alone exhibited an absorption peak at 420 nm, reflecting its limited response to visible light. The incorporation of indium sulfide (In2S3), redshifted the absorption wavelength to 448 nm, suggesting improved electron transfer and enhanced visible light absorption. Further addition of CDs shifted the absorption wavelength to 460 nm, indicating that the TiO2-In2S3-CDs PEC biosensor effectively harnesses visible light for photocatalysis. The band gap of the materials was calculated using the following equation:
( α
h v
)
1 / 2 = A
(
h v
-
E g
)
where A is a constant, α is the absorption coefficient, ℎv is the photon energy, and Eg is the band gap [24]. Pure TiO2 had a band gap of 3.04 eV. After incorporating In2S3, the band gap decreased to 2.78 eV, and it further reduced to 2.61 eV with the addition of CDs (Fig. 4b) [25]. The reduction in band gap is attributed to the formation of Ti-O and Ti-S bonds, which alter the electronic structure and enhance the composite’s response to visible light, thereby improving photocatalytic performance.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photocatalytic mechanism and functional group analysis
ce:section.ce:para: In order to explain the photocatalytic electron transfer mechanism in the TiO2-In2S3-CDs composites, we speculated on the electron transfer process (Fig. 4c). Illuminationoccurs, the functional groups of CDs provided free electrons, which capture oxygenmolecule near the carbon dots during the transfer process to form •O2 − (Fig. S4a). In2S3 generated electron-hole pairs to facilitate the electrons transfer to the conduction band edge of TiO2. Then free electrons near TiO2 combinedwith H2O to generate •OH (Fig. S4b). After the above electron transfer process, the ternary composite material to generated photocathode current [26].

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was employed to analyze the elemental composition and functional groups of the materials (Fig. S5a). The XPS survey spectrum further confirmed the presence of C, O, N, In, Ti, and S in the composite, with all data calibrated to C 1s peak at 284.8 eV. Specifically, The S 2p spectrum showed two peaks at 161.88 eV and 162.48 eV, corresponding to InS and TiS bonds, respectively (Fig. S5b). The Ti 2p spectrum represented two characteristic peaks at 451 eV and 458 eV corresponded to Ti 2p3/2 and Ti 2p1/2, respectively, confirming the presence of Ti4+ (Fig. S5c) [27]. These findings suggested a successful combination of titanium dioxide and indium sulfide, which facilitated efficient electron transfer between the two materials. The C 1s spectrum revealed peaks at 284.49 eV, 285.1 eV, and 289.3 eV, corresponding to CC, CO, and CO bonds, respectively (Fig. S5d) [28]. These peaks indicated the presence of oxygen-containing functional groups that enhanced the photocatalytic activity. In the In 3d spectrum, peaks at 445.18 eV and 452.73 eV corresponded to the 3d5/2 and 3d3/2 tracks of In, respectively (Fig. S5e). The O 1s spectrum showed peaks at 529.81 eV and 531.94 eV, corresponding to TiO and SO bonds, further indicating successful integration of the components. The addition of In2S3 and CDs modified the surface properties of TiO2, increasing oxygen vacancies that may serve as reactive oxygen species (ROS) in photocatalytic processes, thereby enhancing the material's catalytic efficiency (Fig. S5f). The N 1s spectrum, with peaks at 400.8 eV and 401.97 eV corresponding to pyridine N and pyrrole N, respectively (Fig. S5g), indicated the presence of nitrogen species that contribute to the reduced band gap, corroborated by the UV–ViS results [29]. This reduction in band gap facilitated greater photoelectric catalysis, making our PEC biosensor highly effective for applications such as AFP detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Impact of quantum yield on photocatalytic properties of CDs
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate how QY affects the photocatalytic properties of CDs, we synthesized CDs under various temperature conditions and measured their QYs (Table 1). A direct correlation was observed between the photocurrent intensity and the QY of CDs (Fig. 5 a). This correlation indicated that a higher QY enhanced photocatalytic performance, likely due to more efficient photon absorption and generation of electron-hole pairs.

XPS was conducted to analyze the surface chemistry of four different CDs, with a particular focus on CDs 3, which exhibited the highest current (Fig. 5a). XPS tests were performed on CDs 3, and the composites included C, N, O, S (Fig. S6a). In the C 1s XPS spectrum of CDs 3, the binding energy at 283 eV corresponded to CO bond, while peaks at 283.9 eV, 284.8 eV, and 287.2 eV corresponded to CC, CO, and CN/CS bonds, respectively (Fig. S6b) [30]. The N 1s spectrum showed peaks at 398.5 eV and 400.7 eV, corresponding to pyridine N and pyrrole N, respectively (Fig. S6c). The presence of these nitrogen species indicated that CDs possessed conductive properties and provided free electrons, which were essential for enhancing photocatalytic activity. The S 2p spectrum confirmed the doping of nitrogen and sulfur, facilitating charge carrier generation and transfer (Fig. S6d) [31]. The O 1s spectrum showed peaks at 531.5 eV and 533.01 eV, corresponding to CO and CO bonds, respectively, indicating a high abundance of oxygen-containing groups in CDs (Fig. S6e).Comparative XPS spectra of CDs 1, CDs 2, and CDs 4 revealed consistent types of functional groups across samples, though their relative abundances varied (Figs. S7–S9). This variation in functional group concentrations was a significant factor influencing QY and photocatalytic efficiency.

Further analysis combining XPS data from all four CDs showed a trend where higher surface concentrations of oxygen (CO/CO) and nitrogen groups were associated with higher QY (Fig. 5b) [32]. This correlation suggested that these functional groups significantly influenced radiative transitions, thereby affecting the QY of CDs. Specifically, the electron-donating nature of these oxygen groups promoted electron transfer and reduced the band gap, while pyridine nitrogen enhances the graphitization of CDs, further lowering the band gap. The N 1s XPS spectrum demonstrated that the emission peak of pyrrole N at 400.64 eV was most prominent in CDs exhibiting the highest QY (Fig. S10) [33]. As QY increased, the emission peak of pyrrole N progressively redshifted, reinforcing the hypothesis that CDs were moving closer to the visible light range. This shift enhanced the photocatalytic performance, making CDs more effective in photoelectric applications, consistent with the observed experimental results. Zeta potential measurements of the four CDs indicated that all CDs carried negative surface charges, with the highest zeta potential observed for CDs synthesized at 180 °C (Fig. 5c) [34]. This higher negative charge was likely due to the increased presence of surface oxygen groups and graphite nitrogen, which aligned with the XPS data and further supported the enhanced photocatalytic performance of CDs with higher QY.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical and EIS testing of the PEC biosensor
ce:section.ce:para: The performance of the photoelectrochemical biosensor was further assessed through electrochemical testing and EIS (Fig. 6 ). As shown in Fig. 6a, the TiO2 electrode alone exhibited a weak photocurrent, reflecting its limited electron transfer capability [35]. The introduction of In2S3 significantly enhanced the photocurrent, demonstrating its effectiveness in promoting electron transfer. The incorporation of CDs further improved the photocurrent due to their electron-donating properties, which increased visible light absorption and overall photocatalytic activity. Notably, the presence of nitrogen (N) and sulfur (S) elements contributed to the enhanced photoelectrochemical (PEC) performance of the biosensor.

When the biosensor's surface was altered by alpha-fetoprotein (AFP) aptamer and bovine serum albumin (BSA), the photocurrent decreased [36]. This reduction was attributed to the steric hindrance caused by the aptamer and BSA, which obstructed the movement of the electrolyte on the electrode surface, thereby reducing charge transfer. Conversely, the introduction of AFP led to a subsequent increase in photocurrent. This increase likely resulted from the accumulation of hole-electron pairs, indicating the effective operation of the PEC biosensor for AFP detection. Electrochemical Impedance Spectroscopy (EIS) measurements revealed a correlation between impedance and current. Specifically, an increase in impedance was associated with a decrease in current, as illustrated by the larger radius of the Nyquist plot (Fig. 6b) [37]. This finding corroborated the photocurrent data, confirming that higher impedance corresponds to lower photocurrent, thereby validating the biosensor's ability to detect AFP through changes in impedance and photocurrent response.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Performance evaluation of the TiO2-In2S3-CDs PEC biosensor for AFP detection
ce:section.ce:para: The TiO2-In2S3-CDs biosensor was employed for the detection of AFP, demonstrating a linear detection range from 0.002 to 100 ng/mL (Fig. S11a). The biosensor exhibited a negative correlation between AFP concentration and photocurrent response, with the photocurrent decreasing as AFP concentration increased. A standard calibration curve was established by plotting the change in photocurrent (ΔI) against the logarithm of AFP concentration. Here, ΔI represents the difference between the initial photocurrent intensity (I 0) and the photocurrent intensity after AFP incubation (I) (Fig. S11b) [38]. The relationship between the AFP concentration and the photocurrent change was linear over the tested range, with a regression equation of (μA) = 3.53022 * log (CAFP ng/mL) − 0.03425, with a high correlation coefficient of R 2 = 0.9992, indicating an excellent fit. The biosensor achieved a detection limit of 5.1 pg/mL (S/N = 3), underscoring its high sensitivity and capability to detect low AFP concentrations [39]. This performance demonstrated the biosensor's ability to effectively suppress photocurrent recombination, thereby enhancing sensitivity and achieving a low detection limit.

To evaluate the practical applicability of the PEC biosensor, the spiked recovery method was employed to detect AFP in human serum samples. Without additional AFP spiking, the biosensors detected an AFP concentration of approximately 1.25 ng/mL in standard human serum. The recovery rates in spiked samples ranged from 98.2 % to 100.6 %, with relative standard deviation (RSD) between 3.75 % and 4.89 %, confirming the biosensor's accuracy and reliability in complex biological matrices (Table 2 ). Each serum sample was tested in triplicate to ensure precision and consistency. These results confirm that the TiO2-In2S3-CDs PEC biosensor is both accurate and reliable for detecting AFP in complex biological matrices [40].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity, anti-interference, stability, and reproducibility testing of the TiO2-In2S3-CDs PEC biosensor
ce:section.ce:para: To assess the selectivity and specificity of the TiO2-In2S3-CDs PEC biosensor, we tested the biosensor's response to various potentially interfering substances, including PSA, CEA, IgG and AA (Fig. S12a). The biosensor displayed minimal changes in photocurrent when exposed to these interfering agents, even at concentrations of 10 ng/mL. In contrast, a strong photocurrent response to AFP was observed, demonstrating the biosensor's excellent selectivity and specificity for AFP. Anti-interference capability was further assessed by mixing AFP (10 ng/mL) with PSA, CEA, IgG, or AA (each at 10 ng/mL) (Fig. S12b). The photocurrent response remained stable, indicating that the biosensor effectively resisted non-specific adsorption and maintained accurate detection in the presence of potential interfering substances, confirming the biosensor's robust anti-interference properties [41]. Biosensor stability was evaluated through 10 cycles of light on/off switching over 400 s (Fig. S12c). The photocurrent response remained consistent across all cycles, indicating high stability and reliable performance over repeated use. Additionally, reproducibility was assessed by preparing five independent TiO2-In2S3-CDs PEC biosensors under identical conditions and storing them at 4 °C. Weekly tests over four weeks showed that the photocurrent response at 100 ng/mL AFP remained at 95.4 % of the initial value, with an RSD of 0.96 % (Fig. S12d). This demonstrated excellent reproducibility and consistent performance over time, further validating the biosensor's reliability for long-term applications [42].

In conclusion, this study presents the successful development of a TiO2-In2S3-CDs PEC biosensor, optimized for the sensitive and selective detection of AFP. The integration of TiO2, In2S3, and CDs resulted in a biosensor with enhanced photocatalytic activity, attributed to the improved photon absorption and electron-hole pair generation due to the optimized CDs. The biosensor exhibited high sensitivity and accuracy for AFP detection. It also showed excellent selectivity over potential interferents, with reliable performance in human serum and stability over multiple cycles. Its high sensitivity, selectivity, and robustness make it a promising tool for AFP detection and potentially clinical diagnostics.

The work developed a TiO2-In2S3-CDs PEC sensor for highly stable and sensitive detection of AFP. The impact mechanism quantum yield of CDs on the photoelectric properties was also explored. Finally, the sensor was successfully used in the actual samples.

Hongxu Zhao: Writing – original draft, Investigation, Conceptualization. Haiyan Qi: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization. Tonghui Yi: Funding acquisition. Tao Jing: Funding acquisition, Data curation. Jun Li: Supervision, Formal analysis. Siqi Shen: Software. Qingxin Zeng: Software. Yang Gao: Software.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The work is funded by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (52172092); the Fundamental Research Funds in Heilongjiang Provincial Universities (145309525); the Special Scientific Research Fund of Powerful Province Through Higher Education (GJQSCYH-2022001); the Characteristic Discipline of Plant Foods Processing Technology Research Project (Grant No. YSTSXK202307); the Qiqihar city Science and Technology Programme of Joint Guidance Project (Grant No. LHYD-202013).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2025.113115.

ce:section.ce:para:
